Bias Mimicking is introduced as a novel resampling method that bridges the performance gap between sampling and nonsampling methods, showing competitive results on benchmarks compared to non-sampling state-of-the-art approaches.
Bias Mimicking improves the average under-represented subgroups accuracy by more than 3% compared to other sampling methods, showcasing its effectiveness in mitigating bias.
An extensive empirical analysis of Bias Mimicking details the method's sensitivity to the Mimicking condition, providing insights into its behavior and effectiveness in mitigating bias.

To train an image classification model on the resulting distributions, we binarize each and feed it through a dedicated binary prediction head.
Alternatively, we could dedicate a multi-class prediction head for each distribution. However, this alternative will introduce significantly more parameters.
Put together, the binary prediction heads are equivalent #parameters wise to a model that uses one multi-class prediction head.

To perform inference, we can not use the scores from the binary predictors because they may not be calibrated with respect to each other. Therefore, we train a multi-class prediction head using the debiased feature encoder from Bias Mimicking and freeze the gradient from flowing back to ensure the bias is not relearned.

In bias mitigation, the goal is to prevent spurious correlations between predictions of samples and their memberships in bias groups.
Spurious correlations occur when samples of a target class are over-represented by one bias group rather than distributed equally among dataset bias groups.
The distribution of training data can lead to spurious correlations if one bias group is significantly over-represented for a target class.
Our work addresses methods that fix spurious correlations by ensuring statistical independence between Y and B on the dataset level.
In that spirit, we introduce a new sampling method: Bias Mimicking in Section 3.1.
Some of the popular sampling solutions in class imbalance literature that could also be applied to Bias Mitigation are missing from benchmarks used in prior work.
Therefore, we briefly review the missing methods in Section 3.2 and describe how Bias Mimicking addresses their shortcomings in Bias Mitigation.
The goal in our method is to decorrelate a model’s predictions of samples ˆY from their bias group membership B.
Simple sampling methods like Oversampling, Undersampling, and Upweighting need to be improved as discussed in the introduction.
We address these shortcomings in our proposed sampling algorithm: Bias Mimicking.

Dc is used to train the model. The model is trained such that it sees each dc through a different prediction head to prevent the loss function from taking gradients of repetitive copies and risking overfitting.

Dc can be dedicated to a multi-class prediction head for each dc. However, this choice introduces additional prediction heads compared to a model that uses only one. To avoid this, each dc is binarised and then used to train a one-vs-all binary classifier BPc for each c.
The model uses different prediction heads to see each dc. Each head is trained on image-target pairs from its corresponding distribution dc as demonstrated in Figure 2.
Binary predictors are used instead of a multi-class head since each binary predictor is one output node, thus introducing no extra parameters compared to a multi-class head.

Gradients of each prediction head are accumulated across iterations, and they are only backpropagated once the classifier batch is full. This helps mitigate the issue of a prediction head seeing only a small number of samples at certain iterations.
